title: Mutual Exclusivity Assumption
concept: The mutual exclusivity assumption asserts that an image can belong to only one class, causing issues when multiple classes co-occur.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the core assumption of single-label classification regarding class labels?
exercises.explain: Correct! Single-label models assume categories are mutually exclusive — only one can be true.

cards.title: The truck carrying a car
cards.body: If you show a single-label vehicle classifier a photo of a flatbed truck carrying a sedan, the model must choose: is it a 'truck' or a 'car'? It cannot say both, forcing an artificial choice.

cards.title: What is Mutual Exclusivity?
cards.body: The mutual exclusivity assumption is the logical constraint that an input image can belong to exactly one class. Mathematically, this is enforced by Softmax, which makes classes compete for probability.

cards.title: A single winner race
cards.body: Think of a race where only one runner can take first place. Even if two runners cross the finish line milliseconds apart, the rules demand only one winner. The runner-up gets nothing.

cards.title: Medical imaging conflict
cards.body: In chest X-rays, a patient might have both pneumonia and cardiomegaly. A single-label classifier would fail to capture both diagnoses, potentially missing a life-threatening condition.

cards.title: Overlapping objects are common
cards.body: You might think most real-world scenes are simple enough for single labels. In reality, almost all natural images contain multiple objects (e.g., a dog sitting on a couch near a window).

cards.title: Enforced Competition
cards.body: How Softmax forces competition between classes:

exercises.prompt: What occurs when you train a single-label classifier on images that actually contain multiple valid classes?
exercises.explain: Because Softmax forces the outputs to sum to 1, the model must suppress all but the highest score, even if multiple are correct.

options.text: The model gets confused and is forced to pick one class, suppressing the others
The model automatically switches to multi-label mode
The loss function automatically becomes zero

exercises.prompt: True or false: An image showing a cat sleeping on a dog violates the mutual exclusivity assumption of single-label classification.
exercises.explain: True. Both cat and dog are present, so a single label cannot fully describe the image correctly.
Softmax normalizes outputs globally, making the probability of each class depend on all others.
exercises.sentence: The mutual exclusivity assumption is mathematically enforced by the ___ function.

exercises.prompt: In which of these scenarios is the mutual exclusivity assumption violated?
exercises.explain: Having both a tree and a house means the image has multiple valid categories, violating single-label assumptions.

options.text: An image contains both a tree and a house, labeled as just 'house'
A handwritten digit photo containing only the number '7'
A close-up photo of a clean red apple labeled 'apple'

exercises.prompt: If you are designing a system to classify satellite images for land cover (forest, urban, water, agriculture) and many images contain both forest and agriculture, what is the best architectural decision?

options.text: Reject the single-label assumption and use multi-label classification.
Use Softmax but double the number of training epochs.
Crop the images so only one land type is visible in each crop.
Convert the input images to grayscale to simplify features.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If classes naturally co-occur in the same image, forcing a single-label assumption is bad design. Switching to multi-label classification is the correct architectural choice. Next, let's look at best practices for single-label training!
